holding: A suspect's invocation of the right to remain silent (like the right to counsel) must be unambiguous; merely staying silent does not invoke it. A waiver may be implied from an uncoerced statement made after a knowing understanding of Miranda rights.
quote: A suspect's Miranda right to counsel must be invoked 'unambiguously.'
point_cited_for: Implicit waiver / a suspect must invoke the right to silence unambiguously too

holding: An ambiguous or equivocal reference to counsel does not require police to stop questioning; the invocation must be clear enough that a reasonable officer would understand it to be a request for an attorney. ('Maybe I should talk to a lawyer' was insufficient.)
quote: if a suspect makes a reference to an attorney that is ambiguous or equivocal in that a reasonable officer in light of the circumstances would have understood only that the suspect might be invoking the right to counsel, our precedents do not require the cessation of questioning
point_cited_for: Ambiguous invocation of counsel does not trigger Edwards

holding: When a suspect invokes the right to remain silent (not counsel), interrogation may later resume on a different offense if the suspect's right to cut off questioning was 'scrupulously honored.' Distinct from the stricter Edwards counsel rule.
quote: the admissibility of statements obtained after the person in custody has decided to remain silent depends... on whether his 'right to cut off questioning' was 'scrupulously honored'

holding: Once an accused invokes the right to counsel, all interrogation must cease and may not resume until counsel is present, unless the accused himself initiates further communication.
quote: an accused... having expressed his desire to deal with the police only through counsel, is not subject to further interrogation by the authorities until counsel has been made available to him, unless the accused himself initiates further communication, exchanges, or conversations with the police
point_cited_for: Once counsel invoked, all interrogation stops until counsel present / accused reinitiates

holding: The Edwards bar is not offense-specific: once a suspect invokes counsel, police may not initiate interrogation about a separate, unrelated offense either.
point_cited_for: Edwards bar extends to interrogation about a separate offense

holding: The Edwards presumption of involuntariness does not last forever: a break in Miranda custody of at least 14 days ends the Edwards bar, after which police may re-Mirandize and reinterrogate. (Shatzer's return to general prison population was a break in custody.)
quote: It seems to us that period is 14 days. That provides plenty of time for the suspect to get reacclimated to his normal life, to consult with friends and counsel, and to shake off any residual coercive effects of his prior custody.
point_cited_for: 14-day break-in-custody ends the Edwards bar (NUANCE: widely misunderstood)

holding: Continuous pre-indictment pretrial detention is NOT a Shatzer 'break in custody'; defendant who invoked counsel and remained in custody ~6 months could not be reinterrogated (even on an unrelated out-of-state murder) without an Edwards violation. Repeated Miranda warnings did not cure it.
point_cited_for: Pre-indictment pretrial detention is not a Shatzer break in custody (illustrates the Shatzer nuance)
correction: Manifest gave only '198 A.3d 963'; parallel official cite is 236 N.J. 174. Both correct.

court: Supreme Court of Louisiana (writ denial; Crichton, J., concurring)
holding: Writ-denial concurrence treated the suspect's statement ('why don't you just give me a lawyer dog cause this is not what's up') as an ambiguous/equivocal reference to counsel that did not invoke the right under Davis. ANECDOTE ONLY: this is a one-justice concurrence to a denial of review, not a merits holding.
quote: the defendant's ambiguous and equivocal reference to a 'lawyer dog' does not constitute an invocation of counsel
point_cited_for: 'Give me a lawyer dog' held ambiguous under Davis - the 'lawyer dog' anecdote
correction: Posture clarification: this is a writ denial with a Crichton concurrence, NOT a full opinion. Present as an illustrative anecdote, not binding/precedential.
